abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly changing how researchers in materials science and chemistry discover, organize, and act on scientific knowledge. This paper analyzes a broad set of community-developed LLM applications in an effort to identify emerging patterns in how these systems can be used across the scientific research lifecycle. We organize the projects into two complementary categories: Knowledge Infrastructure, systems that structure, retrieve, synthesize, and validate scientific information; and Action Systems, systems that execute, coordinate, or automate scientific work across computational and experimental environments. The submissions reveal a shift from single-purpose LLM tools toward integrated, multi-agent workflows that combine retrieval, reasoning, tool use, and domain-specific validation. Prominent themes include retrieval-augmented generation as grounding infrastructure, persistent structured knowledge representations, multimodal and multilingual scientific inputs, and early progress toward laboratory-integrated closed-loop systems. Together, these results suggest that LLMs are evolving from general-purpose assistants into composable infrastructure for scientific reasoning and action. This work provides a community snapshot of that transition and a practical taxonomy for understanding emerging LLM-enabled workflows in materials science and chemistry.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive overview and taxonomy of how Large Language Models are being applied to automate and enhance research in materials science and chemistry. It highlights the shift from simple text assistants to complex, multi-agent systems capable of executing laboratory tasks and managing scientific knowledge.
main_problem: The paper aims to analyze and categorize the emerging landscape of Large Language Model (LLM) applications within the fields of materials science and chemistry.
main_result: The authors identify a transition from single-purpose LLM tools to integrated, multi-agent workflows, establishing a taxonomy of 'Knowledge Infrastructure' and 'Action Systems'.
method: The study performs a broad analysis of community-developed LLM applications, organizing them into a practical taxonomy based on their role in the scientific research lifecycle.
model_or_system: The paper does not describe a physical system, but rather a taxonomy of computational workflows involving LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and multi-agent systems.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the impact of LLMs on scientific discovery, presents a taxonomy of applications (Knowledge Infrastructure vs. Action Systems), identifies prominent technological themes, and concludes with a snapshot of the transition toward composable scientific infrastructure.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
